You know, growing up in a big city, you know, that has got to be the ultimate.
For life, huh?
You get it for life?
Oh, yes.
At least, as far as I know.

You haven't checked today.
That's great.
I think we have time for one more question.
Yeah.
So I'm thinking about the Big Bang, and is there another sort of metaphor that you would use instead of the Big Bang?
If the balloon doesn't work and the Big Bang doesn't work, what would be a better metaphor?
Well, I was joking when I said the Big Soup.

And I think that in some sense, though, that when you go back in time, that's about as far as we know that we - the period that we can actually talk about is a period in which things were very, very dense and right on top of each other.
And, you know, it's - I don't think it's going to catch on, to be honest.
For some reason, the big Mulligatawny just doesn't have the same ring.
(LAUGHTER)
A clam chowder of the universe.
It has to be a lumpy soup, because, you know, we have to be able to form things where the lumps are, you know, so that we get to be here eventually.
So, it's not...
Yeah.
Cosmic clam chowder?
Yeah, exactly.
It's...
Cosmic clam chowder.
It can't be consomme.
It has to be more of a clam chowder.
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah.
But it is something that is hard for people to grasp, that you could have something from nothing, that empty space is not empty, really.
Right?
That's right.
I mean, that aspect itself is mindboggling, you know, to begin with, that we can't actually have an empty space.
We have this very busy, boiling turmoil of what we ordinarily would consider to be completely empty.
And, of course, you know, when it comes down to it, even if you get back to that first instant in your description, you call something a Big Bang or a Big Soup, that begs the question, right?
Because it doesn't get to - what we really want to know is, how do we get that soup?
And, you know, I think that maybe one of these - well, we don't know if it's an, you know, endless question.
Mm-hmm.
And that is something everybody's been trying to think about for years.
I mean, everybody's been trying to answer that question, what - you know, where did it all begin?

I almost imagine that it might define what it means to be a human being, that, you know, you walk out of your cave, you know, at night, and you look up at the stars and you find yourself asking that, and Jill's question.
Mm-hmm.
Mm-hmm.

It's...

All right.
Well, we'll leave with that.
Thank you, Saul Perlmutter.
He is a Nobel winner in physics and professor of physics at UC Berkley, also senior scientist at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory.

Thank you both for taking time to be with us today.

From NPR News, this is Day to Day.
Macworld is Mecca for Apple computer fans.
The annual convention in San Francisco takes place in a couple of weeks.
Missing, though, will be Apple leader Steve Jobs.

He will not give his usually highly anticipated keynote speech, and that has led not only to a drop in Apple stock price, but also to lots of questions about the future of Steve Jobs, of Apple, and of the Macworld Convention itself.
Adam Lisagor is an Apple enthusiast, and he's an iPhone application developer, and he will be attending Macworld.
And Adam, Macworld is usually where new products, the iPhone, for example, new products are unveiled.
And I'm just wondering, you know, Steve Jobs usually gives the razzle-dazzle speech unveiling these new products.
Without him there, do you and the other Apple enthusiasts expect that no big news will be made at the convention this year?
I think that's the expectation.
And when the iPhone was announced a couple of years ago, it was a good chance that the rumor mill was correct in that instance.
And then, it was almost explosive when Steve Jobs announced it, and I was up there that year, and it was really kind of just an incredible event to be near.
But the expectation is that because the rumor mill hasn't been buzzing as much as it would be that there's not really much to expect insofar as product announcements.
So, this portends sort of the end of Macworld?
Because Apple has also said that this is the last year it, the company, will attend Macworld.
Macworld is always something to look forward to.
You know, relatives will ask the Apple nerd in the family, what's coming out with Macworld this year?
Should I hold off, you know, on buying the new iPod or something?
Is it the end of Macworld?
I don't know.

I mean, Macworld is also, besides being a place where Steve Jobs gets up on a stage and announces where the company is going, it's also just a good place for people involved in the trade of products surrounding Apple - it's a place for that community to get together and meet up with each other, have fun, sort of nerd out.
Well, nerd out with me for a minute, if you would, and tell me a little bit about this new iPhone application you're creating and sharing at Macworld.
You're familiar with the micro-blogging service Twitter?
Yeah.
So, Twitter is a really short way of communicating.
It's kind of like instant messaging.
As simple as it sounds, Mayo Clinic John Heit says this is what he does when he flies, and it's really all that's needed.
Researchers theorize that this is probably because of a problem with their limbic system, the part of the brain that regulates emotion.
Somehow, one of the chemicals that signals when to trust and distrust has been disrupted.
This means that it is essentially biologically impossible for kids like Isabelle to distrust - distrust, Jessica says, anyone.
They don't have that kind of evolutionary thing that other kids do, like that - just little twinge of anxiety like, who is this person?
What should I do here?
Am I going to be OK?
They just don't have it.
And so she doesn't have that like, biological warning system.
And for Jessica, Isabelle's mother, there are good things and bad things about parenting a kid like this.
For instance, when Isabelle was little, she was chronically happy.
She smiled at anything.
She loved everyone: family, friends, strangers.
All it took for her is like, the barest beginning of a grin, and then she'd reach out for them to hold her.
And you know, the people who didn't know her - like at the grocery store - just thought, oh, she's so adorable.
And in those days, Jessica says, she and her family were basically tolerant of Isabelle's trusting and loving nature.
We would try to restrain her, but it was somewhat half-heartedly, because I think we were - we didn't want to embarrass her by kind of calling her on the carpet about how open she was.
But as Isabelle got older, the negatives of her trusting nature began to play a larger role.
For example, a couple years ago, Jessica and her family were spending the day at the beach.
She had been begging me to go to Dairy Queen, and I said maybe later.
We'll see.
We'll see.
Well, she had overheard a lady just down the beach telling her kids, OK, let's go to Dairy Queen.
And Isabelle went over and got in the lady's van, got in the backseat, buckled up, and was waiting to be taken to Dairy Queen with that family.
